bull.b: A debt-free balance sheet reduces interest burden and financial distress risk, providing durable optionality to fund trials or restructure financing. With no reported debt across recent years, the company retains strategic flexibility when raising capital or pursuing partnerships over the next 2–6 months.

Marker’s multi-antigen adoptive T‑cell platform targets tumor-escape and applies to hematologic and solid tumors. This structural scientific approach can deliver lasting competitive differentiation if clinical efficacy emerges, supporting durable value creation through platform leverage across indications.

Board-approved expansion of authorized shares materially increases capacity to raise equity or structure strategic deals, a lasting corporate governance tool that supports funding for clinical programs and operations, reducing immediate insolvency risk while enabling multi-quarter development plans.

bear.b: Persistent negative gross profit and sizable operating losses indicate the core business is not yet economically viable. Over the medium term, this undermines margin sustainability and requires sustained external funding until clinical programs translate into revenue-generating products or scalable services.

Consistent negative operating and free cash flow shows the company cannot self-fund R&D or operations. This structural cash burn increases dependency on capital markets, heightens dilution risk, and constrains long-term planning unless clinical progress materially improves funding prospects.

A small, falling revenue base signals weak near-term commercial traction and limits internal resource reinvestment. Without clear trend reversal, scaling operations or funding late-stage trials becomes harder, pressuring margins and making outcomes highly dependent on successful external financing or partnerships.
